title: Colour-wash backgrounds for calligraphy
subtitle: Laying a variegated watercolour wash before the ink goes on
excerpt: A wet-in-wet watercolour wash laid on hot-press paper and dried completely flat creates a painted ground that broad-edge calligraphy sits on rather than into. The sequence is specific: tape the paper, work wet-in-wet with two or three colours, dry flat without applying heat, and confirm the surface is completely cool and dry before any nib touches it. The ink test on a small scrap piece confirms the surface is ready.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from general watercolour technique and calligraphy ink chemistry documented in Edward Johnston, Writing and Illuminating and Lettering (1906): public domain, Internet Archive. Wet-in-wet watercolour technique draws on standard studio practice. No single primary source covers this specific combination; the technique is widely practised and not associated with a single inventor or text.

glossaryTerms.definition: A watercolour technique where a second colour is added while the first is still wet. The two colours blend at their edges without a hard line, creating soft gradients and colour transitions.

Watercolour paper that has been pressed between heated rollers during manufacture, producing a very smooth, hard surface. The smoothness benefits calligraphy over a wash because the nib glides without catching on surface texture.

Warping of wet paper as the fibres absorb water unevenly, causing the surface to curve, ripple, or cockle. Prevented by stretching the paper on a board with tape before wetting; the tape holds the edges while the paper expands and then dries flat.

content.text: Writing calligraphy over a painted wash gives the letterforms a ground that separates them from plain white paper while keeping the overall effect light and open. The order of operations matters: wash first, calligraphy second, and a full dry between them. The common mistake is writing on a surface that still holds a trace of moisture. On damp paper, iron-gall ink feathers into the fibres and the letterforms lose their edges.

items.name: Watercolour paper, hot-press, A4 or A5
items.substitutions: Heavyweight cartridge paper, 220 gsm, works if hot-press is unavailable; ink feathering is more likely on cartridge but manageable with the right ink

items.name: Watercolour paint, half-pan set
items.substitutions: Tube watercolours thinned in the wells of a palette; a limited palette of two to three colours produces cleaner blends

items.name: Flat wash brush, 50 mm
items.substitutions: Any wide, soft-bristle brush; a house-painting brush works for very large sheets

items.name: Board for stretching (smooth plywood or MDF)
items.substitutions: A clipboard works for small sheets

items.name: Masking tape or gummed paper tape
items.substitutions: Gummed tape provides a stronger hold than masking tape on very wet sheets

items.name: Mitchell Roundhand nib (optional, for writing on the finished wash)

content.text: Tape the paper to a board before wetting it. Tear four strips of masking tape and press them firmly along all four edges of the paper, overlapping the edge of the board by at least 10 mm. The tape anchors the edges while the paper fibres expand when wetted. Without this step, the sheet buckles as it absorbs water and dries with a corrugated surface that makes calligraphy impossible.

Mix the watercolour in a well or palette. Aim for a consistency slightly more saturated than a standard watercolour wash: the colour should be visibly strong on the palette but not opaque. Transparent or semi-transparent watercolour colours work best. Opaque pigments (cadmium yellow, Chinese white) tend to sit on the surface and can leave a slightly chalky ground that accepts ink unevenly.

Wet the surface of the paper with clean water before applying colour. Load the flat brush with water and work across the sheet in horizontal strokes, covering the whole area. The surface should be evenly shiny with no dry patches. This initial wetting prevents the first colour stroke from creating a hard edge where it meets dry paper.

. Load the brush with the first colour and lay strokes across the wet surface. Work in one direction only; do not scrub back and forth. Do not aim for even coverage. Uneven, variegated colour is the goal: areas of concentration next to near-clear areas. The wet surface will pull the colour slightly further than the brush stroke, softening the edges naturally.

Add the second colour immediately, while the first is still visibly wet. Load the brush with the second colour and place strokes where you want the blend to happen. The two colours will merge at their edges. Tilt the board gently (15 to 20 degrees) to encourage the colour to flow in one direction. This tilt creates a natural gradient from one end of the sheet to the other.

Set the board flat and allow the wash to dry completely at room temperature. Do not use a heat gun or hair-dryer: the forced hot air blisters the paper surface and drives the wet colour into irregular tide-marks. Natural drying takes 30 to 60 minutes depending on the humidity and the weight of the paper. The
you see in the first 10 minutes will resolve as the sheet dries under the tape.

Test the surface before writing. Press the back of the sheet at the centre: if the area feels even slightly cool compared to the edges and corners, the paper is still holding moisture in the middle. Wait longer. Once the surface is uniformly warm (or room temperature throughout), do the ink test: rule a few guide lines on a scrap piece of the same paper, wash a small section with your leftover colour, allow it to dry, then write a line of calligraphy across it. If the letterforms hold crisp edges with no feathering, the main sheet is ready.
Write the calligraphy on the dried

surface. Foundational and Italic work well; the smooth surface accepts broad-edge nibs cleanly. Copperplate and other pointed-pen styles are more problematic: the pointed nib catches the slight tooth that even hot-press paper retains after a wash, and the hairlines tend to skip. If you want pointed-pen calligraphy on a wash background, test on the scrap piece first and adjust ink viscosity (thicker ink flows more reliably under these conditions).

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Most failures with wash backgrounds come from one of two sources: not stretching the paper first, or writing before the surface is fully dry.

items.symptom: The paper buckled into permanent ridges even after drying
items.cause: The paper was not taped down before wetting, or the tape was insufficient.
items.fix: For the current sheet: while still slightly damp, place between sheets of blotting paper under a heavy flat weight for several hours. For future sheets: tape all four edges before any water touches the surface.

items.symptom: The ink feathers into the wash surface
items.cause: The wash surface was not fully dry. Even a trace of moisture causes ink to wick into the paper fibres.
items.fix: Allow at least an hour more. Check the back of the sheet. If the paper still feels cool, it is still drying.

items.symptom: The colours dried with harsh tide-marks and blotchy edges
items.cause: The wash was applied with too much back-and-forth scrubbing, or the second colour was added after the first had started to dry rather than while still wet.
items.fix: Work faster on the initial wetting and the first colour pass. Wet-in-wet requires the surface to be visibly shiny throughout; once the sheen fades, the blending window is closed.

content.text: A wash background adds colour to calligraphy work without the complexity of gum ammoniac size or coloured ink. Once the wet-in-wet technique is reliable, two next steps are worth exploring. The first is masking areas before the wash goes on, to preserve white space for a letterform added later. The second is using the wash as a ground for a wax-resist pattern beneath the colour.
